app.title: Learn Signal Chain
app.resetConfirm: Reset all controls to default values?

beginner.title: Beginner
beginner.description: Simple chain: microphone → preamp → EQ → compressor → speaker.

intermediate.title: Intermediate
intermediate.description: Full chain including channel fader and master bus.

advanced.title: Advanced
advanced.description: Full chain with all processing stages.

levels.switchConfirm: Switch to {title}? This will reset all controls to defaults.

warnings.preampTooQuiet: Preamp output is too quiet. Increase gain to bring the signal into the green zone.
warnings.preampClipping: Preamp is clipping! Reduce the gain — you are adding distortion before anything else can help.
warnings.eqClipping: EQ is boosting the signal into clipping. Reduce EQ boost or lower preamp gain.
warnings.heavyCompression: Heavy compression: {amount} dB of gain reduction. The signal may sound pumped or squashed.
warnings.masterClipping: Master output is clipping. Lower the fader or master trim to prevent distortion.
warnings.masterTooQuiet: Master output is too quiet. Increase gain earlier in the chain.
warnings.domainMixedBus: Cannot mix analog and digital signals — insert a DAC before this bus, or keep signals in the same domain.
warnings.digitalToAmp: Amp cannot amplify a digital signal. Insert a DAC before the amp to convert back to analog.
warnings.digitalToSpeaker: Speaker cannot receive a digital signal. Insert a DAC before the speaker.
warnings.adcExpectsAnalog: ADC expects an analog input. Connect it after analog gear, not after another ADC or digital node.
warnings.dacExpectsDigital: DAC expects a digital input. Connect it after an ADC or other digital node.

eq.label: Parametric EQ
eq.graphHint: Drag handles on graph · Bell = peak boost/cut · Shelf = tilt all highs or lows

speaker.label: Speaker / Monitor
speaker.needsAmp: Needs a power amplifier — connect an Amp node before this speaker

di-box.label: DI Box
di-box.description: Converts high-impedance instrument signal to balanced mic-level XLR.

eqCurve.title: EQ Curve
eqCurve.subtitle: Drag the colored handles to shape the frequency response

mic.what: The microphone converts sound (acoustic energy) into an electrical signal. It outputs a very weak voltage — typically around -60 dBu for a dynamic microphone.
mic.why: Everything starts here. The quality and placement of the microphone determines the raw material for the entire signal chain. A good source signal makes everything downstream easier.
mic.tip: The signal is in the blue (too quiet) zone — that is completely normal and expected at this stage. The preamp's job is to fix that.

line-in.what: A line input accepts signals that are already at "line level" — roughly -10 dBu for consumer gear, +4 dBu for professional gear. This is the standard level used between devices: from a keyboard, audio interface, or effects unit into a mixer.
line-in.why: Line-level signals are strong enough to drive mixing and processing gear directly without a preamp. They sit well above the noise floor and well below the clipping point.
line-in.tip: If your line-level signal looks green (-40 to -12 dBu) you are good to go. If it is too quiet, the source device may have its own output level control — adjust that first before adding gain in the chain.

instrument.what: An instrument input (also called "Hi-Z" or high impedance) is designed for direct connection of guitars, basses, and similar passive pickups. The signal is louder than a microphone but still below true line level — typically -30 to -10 dBu.
instrument.why: Guitar pickups have a high output impedance. Connecting them to a standard line input or mic input causes tone loss (the high frequencies get loaded down). An instrument input has the correct impedance — 1 MΩ or more — to let the signal pass without colouring it.
instrument.tip: Going direct (DI) from an instrument bypasses the amp entirely. This is common in recording and live sound. A DI box can also be used to convert the high-impedance signal to a low-impedance balanced mic-level signal, which travels well over long cables.

gain.what: The preamp/gain stage boosts the input signal up to a usable working level. For a microphone, this can be +40 to +60 dB of gain. For a line-level source, you may only need +10 to +20 dB.
gain.why: Without enough gain here, all downstream processors work with a weak signal and introduce more noise. Too much gain causes clipping before anything else in the chain can help. Getting this stage right is the foundation of gain staging.
gain.tip: Aim for the output to land in the green zone (−40 to −12 dBu). This is called gain staging, and it starts right here.

preamp.what: The preamplifier (preamp) boosts the weak microphone-level signal up to "line level" — strong enough for the rest of the chain to work with. Typical gain: +20 to +60 dB.
preamp.why: Without enough gain here, all downstream processors — EQ, compressor, fader — work with a weak signal and introduce more noise. Too much gain causes distortion before anything else can help.
preamp.tip: Aim for the output to land in the green zone (-40 to -12 dBu). This is called gain staging, and it starts right here at the preamp.

hpf.what: The High-Pass Filter (HPF) removes frequencies below a chosen cutoff point. Everything below that frequency is rolled off — "blocked". Everything above it "passes" through unchanged.
hpf.why: Microphones and instruments pick up low-frequency rumble: air conditioning, footsteps, handling noise, mic stand vibration. These frequencies serve no musical purpose and just muddy the mix. The HPF cleans them up before they reach the EQ or compressor.
hpf.tip: For most vocals start at 80–120 Hz. For acoustic guitar, try 100–150 Hz. Never use HPF on a kick drum or bass — you'll cut the fundamental frequency you actually want. The curve display shows exactly which frequencies you're removing.

eq.what: The Parametric EQ shapes the tonal character of the signal by boosting or cutting specific frequency ranges. Each band targets a frequency area: Low (body), Mid (presence), High (air and clarity). Bell mode shapes a peak or dip; Shelf mode boosts or cuts everything above/below a corner frequency.
eq.why: Every sound source has frequencies that help or hurt its purpose. A vocal may need less boxiness around 400 Hz, more presence at 3 kHz, and more air at 10 kHz. The EQ lets you sculpt that precisely.
eq.tip: EQ is mostly subtractive in professional practice — cut what you don't need rather than boosting everything. Boosting adds energy; cutting cleans up the mix. In Intermediate mode you can switch Low and High bands to Shelf for a smoother, more musical result.

graphic-eq.what: The Graphic EQ divides the spectrum into fixed frequency bands (here: 10 bands, one per octave from 31 Hz to 16 kHz). Each band has its own fader — push it up to boost that octave, pull it down to cut.
graphic-eq.why: Graphic EQs are found on the master output of PA systems as the final tool for correcting room acoustics — a sudden peak at 4 kHz, a resonant boom at 63 Hz. They are less precise than parametric EQs but faster to use for broad shaping.
graphic-eq.tip: In live sound the graphic EQ on the master output is used mainly to prevent feedback and correct for the room, not to shape the mix. Keep moves gentle — small cuts (−3 to −6 dB) solve most problems. Large boosts cause feedback and distortion.

comp.what: The compressor reduces the dynamic range of the signal — it automatically turns down loud peaks and can be used with makeup gain to raise the overall level. It makes quiet parts relatively louder and loud parts relatively quieter.
comp.why: Live performances and recordings have wide dynamic ranges. Without compression, loud notes clip and quiet notes get lost. Compression makes the signal more consistent and controlled.
comp.tip: Watch the gain reduction meter. 2-6 dB of gain reduction is transparent and musical. More than 10 dB starts to sound pumped and squashed — which can be an effect in itself.

fader.what: The channel fader controls the volume of this signal as it enters the mix. Unity gain (0 dB) means the signal passes through unchanged. Moving it down attenuates the signal.
fader.why: Every instrument in a mix needs to sit at the right level relative to everything else. The fader is how you set that balance. It is the primary mixing control.
fader.tip: In a mix, most faders end up below unity. The art of mixing is in the relative balance between all channels, not in making each one as loud as possible.

amp.what: A power amplifier takes a line-level signal and boosts it to speaker-driving level — adding significant current, not just voltage. A passive speaker (one without a built-in amp) requires a power amplifier to produce any sound.
amp.why: Line-level signals are powerful enough for mixing and processing, but not powerful enough to physically move a speaker cone. The power amp provides the current needed to do the work.
amp.tip: Power amps are rated in watts. For studio monitors you typically need 50–100 W. For live PA systems, 500–2000 W per side is common. More wattage = more headroom before clipping, not necessarily more loudness.

switch.what: A switch (or mute button) is the simplest control in a signal chain. When it is on, the signal passes through unchanged. When it is off, the signal is completely cut — silence.
switch.why: Muting is used to silence a channel between takes, to prevent feedback when a microphone is not in use, or to isolate a track during a mix.
switch.tip: In live sound, muting microphones when not in use prevents bleed, handling noise, and feedback. It is a habit every sound engineer develops quickly.

potentiometer.what: A potentiometer (pot) is a variable resistor used as a volume or level control. It uses an audio taper curve, which matches how human hearing perceives loudness — most of the usable range is in the upper half of the knob rotation.
potentiometer.why: Audio equipment uses pots for volume controls, send levels, trim controls, and effects returns. The audio taper ensures that the control feels linear even though loudness is logarithmic.
potentiometer.tip: The 12 o'clock position (50%) is not unity gain on an audio taper pot — unity is typically at 75% of the rotation. This node models that correctly: full CCW = silence, 75% = 0 dB, full CW = +10 dB.

mono-bus.what: A mono bus collects signals from multiple channels and combines them into a single mono output.
mono-bus.why: Use it to merge several microphones or instruments before sending them to the master bus. Grouping related channels (e.g., all backing vocals) lets you control their combined level with one fader.
mono-bus.tip: Watch the output meter — if it clips after combining many channels, lower the gain on the individual channels feeding into it. Each source adds to the total, so the more you combine the louder it gets.

stereo-bus.what: A stereo bus collects signals from multiple channels and combines them into a stereo (Left/Right) output, preserving any left-right positioning set by pan knobs upstream.
stereo-bus.why: Use it to collect a group of sources — like all your drum tracks — and control their combined stereo level before sending everything to the master bus.
stereo-bus.tip: Connect pan nodes before the stereo bus so each source sits correctly in the stereo field. The left and right meters show you the final balance of the group.

stereo-fader.what: A stereo fader has separate Left and Right inputs and outputs. It applies the same level adjustment to both channels simultaneously, so the stereo image stays intact.
stereo-fader.why: Use it to ride the level of a stereo source — a keyboard, a stereo effects return, or the output of a stereo bus — without collapsing it to mono.
stereo-fader.tip: Connect the L Out and R Out of a pan knob or stereo bus into the L In and R In here. Moving the fader down attenuates both channels equally, preserving the left-right balance.

balance.what: A balance control adjusts the relative loudness between the Left and Right channels of a stereo signal. At centre, both channels pass through equally. Turning left fades out the right channel; turning right fades out the left channel.
balance.why: Useful when a stereo source sounds off-centre — for example, a recording where the guitarist is too far left — nudge the balance to compensate without re-mixing the source.
balance.tip: Balance is different from pan: pan positions a mono signal, balance shifts an already-stereo signal. Keep it near centre unless you specifically need to compensate for an off-centre stereo source.

master-bus.what: The master bus is the final summing point where all channels are combined before the signal leaves the system. Every fader move, every processing decision, ultimately ends up here.
master-bus.why: The master bus controls the overall output level. It is also the place for final mix bus processing — subtle compression or limiting to control peaks and add cohesion.
master-bus.tip: Leave headroom on the master bus — ideally keep peaks below −6 dBu. This gives a mastering engineer (or limiter) room to work. A master bus that is already at 0 dBu has nowhere to go.

master.what: The master bus receives all channel signals mixed together. It is the final stage before the signal leaves the system. A small trim here adjusts the overall output level.
master.why: The master bus is where your final mix level is controlled. It also applies any final processing — in professional studios, subtle compression and limiting are applied here to glue the mix.
master.tip: Leave some headroom on the master bus — ideally peaks should not exceed -6 dBu. This leaves room for a mastering engineer (or limiter) to do their job without distortion.

speaker.what: A passive speaker converts electrical signal back into sound, but it has no built-in amplifier. It needs a separate power amplifier (Amp node) to drive it — connecting one directly to line level will produce no sound.
speaker.why: Passive speakers are the standard in live sound PA systems and hi-fi setups. The amplifier is a separate unit chosen for its power output and impedance matching to the speaker.
speaker.tip: Connect an Amp node before this speaker. The amplifier provides the current needed to physically move the speaker cone. Without it, the signal is there but nothing moves.

active-speaker.what: An active (powered) speaker has a built-in amplifier. It accepts line-level signals directly from a mixer, audio interface, or any line-level output — no separate power amp needed.
active-speaker.why: Active speakers are the standard in modern studio monitors and portable PA setups. The built-in amplifier is matched to the speaker driver, which is why powered monitors tend to have more consistent, accurate sound.
active-speaker.tip: The volume knob adjusts the final output level. Good gain staging means getting a healthy green signal to this speaker — then use the volume knob to set how loud you want to listen, not to compensate for a too-quiet or too-loud chain.

di-box.what: A DI box (Direct Injection) converts a high-impedance unbalanced instrument signal — like a guitar or keyboard — into a low-impedance balanced signal that travels cleanly over long cables to a mixing desk. It has two outputs: an XLR output (balanced, for the preamp) and a Direct Output (instrument-level thru, for connecting to an amplifier at the same time).
di-box.why: Guitar pickups and keyboards have a high output impedance. Running them directly into a mixer causes high-frequency tone loss and picks up interference over long cables. The DI box solves both: it presents the correct high-impedance load to the instrument and converts the signal to a noise-resistant balanced format.
di-box.tip: The Ground Lift switch disconnects the cable screen at the DI box end, which breaks ground loops — a common cause of low-frequency hum. If you hear a 50 or 60 Hz buzz, try switching ground lift on.

noise-gate.what: A noise gate is a switch controlled by level. When the signal rises above the threshold, the gate opens and the signal passes through unchanged. When the signal falls below the threshold, the gate closes and the output is silence.
noise-gate.why: Every microphone and cable picks up some background noise — air conditioning hum, electrical interference, room ambience. Between musical phrases, when the performer is quiet, that noise is audible. The gate simply closes during those quiet moments, removing the noise before it reaches the mix.
noise-gate.tip: Set the threshold just above the noise floor and just below the quietest musical content. Too high, and the gate clips the start of notes (chatter). Too low, and the noise gets through. The OPEN/CLOSED indicator shows you exactly when the gate fires.

limiter.what: A limiter is a compressor with an extremely high ratio — effectively infinite. It sets a hard ceiling: no signal can exceed the threshold, no matter how loud the input gets. Below the ceiling, the signal passes through unchanged.
limiter.why: Limiters protect equipment and listeners from sudden loud peaks. A drum hit, a mic bump, a loud vocal phrase — without limiting, any of these can clip the mix bus, damage amplifiers, or cause hearing damage at high volumes. The limiter is the safety net at the end of the chain.
limiter.tip: Unlike a compressor, a limiter is meant to be invisible — you should not hear it working. If you see the 'Limiting' indicator light up often, the signal going into the limiter is too hot. Fix it earlier in the chain rather than relying on the limiter to tame a consistently loud signal.

deesser.what: A de-esser is a frequency-selective compressor. It listens for harsh sibilance — the sharp hissing sounds of letters like 'S', 'SH', and 'CH' in vocal recordings — and automatically reduces only those frequencies when they get too loud. You set the frequency and threshold; the de-esser does the rest automatically.
deesser.why: Microphones and certain processing (especially EQ boosts in the 5–10 kHz range) can exaggerate sibilance until it becomes painful to listen to. A broadband compressor would make everything quieter, which is not the goal. The de-esser targets only the problem frequencies, leaving the rest of the voice untouched.
deesser.tip: Set the frequency to where the sibilance is worst — usually 5–8 kHz for most voices, sometimes up to 10 kHz for brighter voices. The gain reduction meter shows how much de-essing is happening. A little (1–3 dB) is transparent. Heavy de-essing (6+ dB) can make voices sound lispy.

relay.what: A relay is an electronic switch that routes your signal to one of two outputs. You choose Output A or Output B — the chosen path gets the signal; the other carries nothing. This is used for A/B routing: for example, sending a guitar to an amp (A) or directly to the interface (B) without unplugging cables.
relay.why: In live sound and recording, you often need to switch between two signal paths instantly — different monitor mixes, parallel processing chains, or backup routes. A relay does this cleanly without noise or clicks.
relay.tip: The active output is highlighted in green; the inactive one is grey. Try connecting Output A to a reverb bus and Output B to a dry speaker to instantly switch between wet and dry monitoring.

pan.what: A pan knob distributes a mono signal between the left and right channels of a stereo mix. Turned fully left, the signal goes only to the left speaker. Fully right, only to the right. In the centre, it is split equally between both — but at −3 dB each, so the total loudness stays the same (equal-power law).
pan.why: In a stereo mix, every instrument has a position in the left-right field. Panning creates the sense of space and separation that makes a mix feel wide and clear. Without panning, everything piles up in the centre and the mix sounds crowded.
pan.tip: Connect the L output and R output of the pan knob to the left and right inputs of the Master Bus separately. The stereo bus will then show different levels for L and R, reflecting where you have placed the sound in the stereo field.

audio-interface.what: An audio interface is the device that connects your studio to your computer. It receives the final analog signal from your mixer or monitor controller, converts it to digital, and sends it to your recording software. In this signal chain, it is the last stop — nothing comes after it.
audio-interface.why: Every recording studio needs a way to get audio into a computer. The audio interface takes the balanced analog outputs from your desk and converts them to digital data that your DAW can record. It also handles the reverse — playing back digital audio from your computer as analog signal for monitoring.
audio-interface.tip: The level reaching the audio interface matters. Aim for a signal that peaks in the green zone (−20 to −12 dBFS in digital terms, roughly −18 to −12 dBu in the analog chain). This gives the converter enough signal to work cleanly while leaving headroom for unexpected peaks.

adc.what: An Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) samples the continuously varying analog voltage from your gear thousands of times per second and converts each measurement into a digital number. The level scale changes from dBu (analog) to dBFS (digital full scale). With the standard alignment of −18 dBu = 0 dBFS, a signal at −18 dBu becomes 0 dBFS.
adc.why: Computers work with numbers, not voltages. The ADC is the bridge between the physical world of microphones and amplifiers and the digital world of DAWs and plugins. Everything after the ADC is math, not electricity.
adc.tip: 0 dBFS is the ceiling in digital audio — there is no louder. Unlike analog gear which can go a few dB over nominal before distorting, digital clips instantly and harshly at 0 dBFS. Aim to keep peaks well below 0 dBFS (−18 to −6 dBFS) to protect against sudden transients.

dac.what: A Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC) does the reverse of an ADC: it reads the digital numbers from your computer and reconstructs a continuously varying analog voltage. Using the same −18 dBu = 0 dBFS alignment, 0 dBFS becomes −18 dBu, and anything after the DAC is back in the analog world.
dac.why: Speakers and amplifiers are analog devices — they respond to voltage, not numbers. The DAC converts the digital mix back into real electrical signal so you can hear it through speakers. Without a DAC, digital audio stays in the computer.
dac.tip: Place the DAC before any analog gear that follows — amplifiers, passive speakers, analog signal processors. The domain indicator on the node turns green (analog) after the DAC, confirming that everything downstream is back in the correct electrical domain.
